cataloging_subject.label: The Vision of Tundale
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English verse version of Tundale's otherworld journey, represented in the repository by U-M's encoding of Albrecht Wagner's 1893 critical text. This cataloging subject excludes Marcus's twelfth-century Latin Visio Tnugdali, each individual manuscript witness, Wagner's modern German introduction and commentary, and U-M's OCR/TEI encoding as distinct lineage layers.

catalog_summary.title: The Vision of Tundale
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.region: England; rhyme-based Northern English dialect localization; narrative set in Ireland

identifiers.scheme: New Index of Middle English Verse

notes: The poem's manuscripts and modern scholarship also use Tondale, Tundal, and related forms.

titles.value: Tundale: Das mittelenglische Gedicht über die Vision des Tundalus

scope.description: Title of Wagner's immediate 1893 source edition

notes: This is an edition-layer title, not evidence that the medieval work was composed in German.
Richard Heeg is the scribe of one later witness, and Marcus is author of the Latin source work; neither should be promoted to author of the anonymous Middle English poem.

scope.description: Translation or vernacular adaptation of the Tundale narrative

notes: The exact immediate source—Latin recension or Anglo-Norman intermediary—remains disputed.

scope.description: Verse reshaping, selection, and mitigation of some source passages
Four-witness critical text with comparison to the Latin source

notes: The Getty authority record gives circa 1400. Wagner argued for the beginning of the fifteenth century, perhaps still the fourteenth; this record does not normalize an approximate date to an exact year.

scope.description: Date assigned within the narrative to Tundale's vision in Ireland

notes: This is a narrated event date inherited from the Latin source, not the Middle English translation date.
This cultural-origin statement does not identify a specific place of composition or copying.

scope.description: Reconstructed language of the poem from rhyme evidence

notes: Wagner's localization is based on rhymes across an eclectic four-witness tradition; none of the four scribal witnesses preserves the reconstructed original dialect unchanged.
The Middle English version identifies Ireland but omits Marcus's more specific Cork setting.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: German apparatus and a Latin witness colophon belong to editorial or manuscript layers and do not make the cataloged medieval work multilingual.

form_statements.description: A narrative poem in short rhyming couplets, often described as octosyllabic but more cautiously read as flexible four-stress lines.

genre_statements.label: Visionary writing
notes: Tundale's apparent death and soul journey make ‘otherworld vision’ more precise than treating the poem simply as a dream vision.

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English verse retelling of the Vision of Tundale. Tundale, a wealthy and violent Irish knight, collapses at a meal and appears dead; separated from his body, his soul is guided by an angel through escalating punishments for sin, Hell and Lucifer, milder purgatorial states, earthly-paradisal joys, and a glimpse of Heaven. Returned to life, he confesses, receives the sacrament, gives away his goods, preaches what he has seen, and lives penitently. The poem makes divine justice, mercy, punishment, and conversion central to its account of the Christian afterlife.

This record describes the medieval Middle English poem. Wagner's German critical apparatus and U-M's OCR/TEI are described as source and encoding layers in the linked lineage manifest; Marcus's Latin Visio Tnugdali is a related source work, not the same cataloging unit.

Wagner's edited poem is continuous rather than divided into stable named sections. The Advocates' witness uniquely organizes the journey as ten Passus, seven Gaudia, and a Reversio Anime; because the other Middle English manuscripts lack those headings, this record does not impose that witness-specific segmentation on the whole work.

extent.description: One continuous verse work in Wagner's edition. The repository XML contains 2,354 L elements and no LG elements; this is an encoding metric for Wagner's eclectic text, not a universal line count across witnesses or modern editions.

related_works.title: Visio Tnugdali
notes: Marcus's Latin work is the secure ultimate narrative source. Whether the Middle English translator used a Latin recension directly or worked through an Anglo-Norman intermediary remains unresolved.

evidence.locator: HEADER; text division; lines 1–2354; apparatus and trailer
evidence.summary: Supplies the encoded Middle English poem, its narrative and ending, Wagner source statement, U-M identifier, editorial apparatus, continuous structure, and the distinction between the 1149 narrative date and the 1451 R witness colophon.

evidence.citation: Element and content inspection of repository aaw7316.xml.
evidence.locator: DLPSTEXTCLASS/DIV1/L, P, PB, and NOTE1 elements
evidence.summary: Counts four DIV1 elements overall, one continuous text division, 2,354 L elements, no LG elements, 188 PB milestones, and 2,208 NOTE1 apparatus entries. Counts describe this encoding rather than every witness or edition.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, CME item AAW7316.0001.001, Tundale.
evidence.summary: Confirms the immediate Wagner 1893 edition, stable identifier, public digital access, title, and contents divisions.

evidence.citation: Albrecht Wagner, ed., Tundale: Das mittelenglische Gedicht über die Vision des Tundalus (Halle an der Saale: Max Niemeyer, 1893), BSB digital facsimile.
evidence.locator: title page; pp. IX–XL; text and apparatus pp. 1–146
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for title, Wagner's editor/collator roles, four-witness method, northern rhyme-based dialect localization, flexible short-couplet form, Latin-source comparison, and approximate dating to the beginning of the fifteenth century or possibly the fourteenth.

evidence.citation: Edward E. Foster, ed., The Vision of Tundale: Introduction and Textual Notes, Middle English Texts Series.
evidence.summary: Supplies the established title, anonymous status, five-witness tradition, Latin ultimate source and probable Vincent recension, witness-specific structure, detailed plot, otherworld/purgatory genre, and theological emphasis on justice and mercy.

evidence.citation: Middle English Texts Series, Vol. 18, The Vision of Tundale, ed. Rodney Mearns (1985).
evidence.summary: Describes the poem as a Middle English verse translation of a Latin prose work, written in octosyllabic couplets and surviving in five mid- or late-fifteenth-century manuscripts.

evidence.citation: J. Paul Getty Trust, CONA Iconography Record 901001219, Vision of Tundale.
evidence.summary: Provides the preferred English title, anonymous English creator, circa-1400 date, England origin, five-witness count, verse-couplet form, and relation to Visio Tnugdali; its Anglo-Norman intermediary claim remains in conflict with Foster's direct-Latin account.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for aaw7316, Wagner edition and witness research.
evidence.summary: Binds the descriptive record to Wagner's 1893 edition, its four manuscript sources, editorial method, and public BSB facsimile.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for aaw7316, Latin source-work research.
evidence.summary: Binds the related-work and 1149 historical-setting claims to Marcus's Latin Visio Tnugdali and distinguishes that source layer from the Middle English poem.

open_questions.question: Was the Middle English poem translated directly from a Latin recension, especially the Vincent of Beauvais tradition, or through an Anglo-Norman intermediary?
next_steps: Consult Eileen Gardiner's 1980 article on the Middle English translation and Rodney Mearns's 1985 parallel Latin edition.
Do not promote either competing route from a note to a definitive relation without textual comparison.

open_questions.question: Can the circa-1400 translation date and Wagner's Northern English rhyme-based localization be refined or revised using current linguistic scholarship?
next_steps: Consult Gardiner's 1982 dating article and Mearns's language chapter.
Keep witness production dates and scribal dialects separate from the reconstructed language of the work.

open_questions.question: Do the ten Passus, seven Gaudia, and Reversio Anime headings in Adv.MS.19.3.1 preserve authorial structure or a witness-specific scribal organization?
next_steps: Compare rubrics and transitions across all five witnesses in Gardiner and Mearns.
Retain summary-level structure until the divisions can be shown to belong to the work rather than one witness.

notes: The repository XML's German language declaration describes the edition's apparatus poorly and does not override direct inspection of the Middle English poem.
The 1149 date belongs to the narrated vision and Latin source tradition; the 1451 colophon dates the Royal witness; 1893 dates Wagner's edition; and 2006 dates U-M's header creation.

The 2,354-line extent is specific to Wagner's eclectic text and this XML representation. Mearns reports 2,320 lines for Cotton, while the Advocates' version printed by Foster has a different lineation.
No single manuscript's scribal dialect, date, rights status, or internal headings are assigned wholesale to the abstract Middle English work.
